content.problemStatement:
You are given two integers LLL and R (L+3≤R)R\: (L+3\leq R)R(L+3≤R). Output any **four distinct** integers between LLL and RRR (inclusive) such that their **bitwise `XOR`** is zero.

More formally, output any **four** integers a1,a2,a3,a4a_1, a_2, a_3, a_4a1​,a2​,a3​,a4​ such that:


a1⊕a2⊕a3⊕a4=0a_1\oplus a_2\oplus a_3\oplus a_4 = 0a1​⊕a2​⊕a3​⊕a4​=0
L≤a1,a2,a3,a4≤RL\leq a_1, a_2, a_3, a_4\leq RL≤a1​,a2​,a3​,a4​≤R
ai=aja_i=a_jai​=aj​ if and only if i=ji=ji=j
If more than one such quadruple exists, you may output **any** of them. If no such quadruple exists, print −1-1−1 instead.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows.
Each test case consists of a single line of input, containing two space-separated integers L,RL, RL,R.


Output Format
For each testcase, output any **four distinct** integers between LLL and RRR such that their **bitwise `XOR`** is zero, or output −1-1−1 in case no such quadruple of four distinct integers exists.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers L and R.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output four distinct integers a1 a2 a3 a4 (separated by spaces) such that L ≤ ai ≤ R and a1⊕a2⊕a3⊕a4 = 0. If no such quadruple exists output -1 on a single line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case the smallest even number ≥1 is 2, but 2+3=5>4, so no quadruple exists. For the second test case the smallest even ≥1 is 2 and 2+3=5≤100, thus 2 3 4 5 works (XOR=0).
The smallest even ≥5 is 6, but 6+3=9>7, therefore no suitable four distinct numbers exist.
L+3≤R is violated, so the interval cannot contain four distinct numbers; the algorithm correctly outputs -1.

solution.approach: The XOR of four consecutive numbers starting with an even number is always zero because (e)⊕(e+1)⊕(e+2)⊕(e+3)=0 for any even e. Therefore we only need to find the smallest even integer e in [L,R]. If e+3 ≤ R we can output e, e+1, e+2, e+3; otherwise no four distinct numbers with XOR zero exist in the interval, so we output -1. This runs in O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(1) per test case, O(T) overall

code.C:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){int T; if(scanf("%d",&T)!=1) return 0; while(T--){ long long L,R; scanf("%lld %lld",&L,&R); long long e = (L%2==0)?L:L+1; if(e+3<=R) printf("%lld %lld %lld %lld\n",e,e+1,e+2,e+3); else printf("-1\n"); } return 0; }
